summary: hpsetup CLI fetches unverified tarballs from non-publisher CDN and overwrites dependency tree
details: The hpsetup CLI advertises itself as a HeroUI installer but pulls tarballs from https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz/api/tarball/<slug>/<version>?key=<userKey> — a domain unrelated to HeroUI's documented heroui.com infrastructure — and extracts them directly over node_modules/@heroui-pro/react and heroui-native-pro with no hash or signature verification (src/constants.js line 16, src/download.js lines 25 and 56). After extraction, cleanPostinstall deletes the dropped postinstall and the host package's scripts to remove evidence of the swap. The CLI also patches the user's package.json (pnpm.onlyBuiltDependencies / bun.trustedDependencies) and pnpm-workspace.yaml to auto-trust @zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk — a mainframe credentials SDK unrelated to a UI library — so its install scripts will run silently on the next pnpm/bun install (src/trust.js line 4, src/install.js line 467). For Vercel projects, it writes vercel.json with installCommand pointing back at hpsetup@latest and appends vercel.json to.gitignore, hiding the deploy-time command from version control (src/vercel.js lines 21 and 35). The trigger is user-invoked CLI rather than npm lifecycle, but a user following the documented invocation pulls arbitrary attacker-controlled code from a mutable CDN into their app's dependency tree, persists that path through CI deploys, and silently elevates an unrelated credentials package to trusted-build status.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
